content_html:
Transactions & ACID
Data Warehousing & ETL
INF10025  Data Management and Analytics  Lecture 11 – Part 2

Multiple databases
The next discussion deals with:
Organisations have multiple databases
Organisations / departments need to query data from multiple databases
This has problems
Access to data
Speed of extracting data from many sources
Complexity of gathering data from many sources
Different table names, column names, formats
Different currencies, measurements, terminologies, meanings
A Data Warehouse is often part of the solution.

A Data Warehouse
A Data Warehouse is typically
A different database to the TPS( Transaction Processing System) usually on a different server
A copy of data from the TPS systems is transferred to the DW
Data is regularly transferred from TPS to DW
Continual   or
Hourly    or
Every 24 hrs
Not all data is transferred from TPS to DW
A DW is not a backup or a mirror database
Only relevant data is transferred to TPS
The transferred data is often slightly transformed, cleaned, aggregated
The meaning of the data remains the same

Business Intelligence & Data
A TPS( Transaction Processing System) is typically designed for Operational / Day to Day users
Most queries contain detailed information gathered from the TPS.
Great for dealing with Customer Sales or Library Loans …
A TPS typically uses a RDBMS
Data is stored across many tables
Foreign Key constraints are enforced
Data Redundancy is minimised
A TPS is excellent for day to day operational use
A TPS is terribly slow for Data Analysts who require aggregated queries spanning billions of rows in many related tables
This is one reason why Data Warehouses are created.

A different approach for Data Warehousing
Organizations are often a collection of heterogeneous TPS systems.
CRM customer-relationship system
Sales and Orders system
Manufacturing
Budgeting and Finance
Uni: Library / Enrolment / Timetabling / Blackboard / Doubtfire…
Imagine a university requirement such as:
Analyse if there is a correlation between a student's Blackboard Usage, Tutorial attendance, Library Usage, Assignment submission dates and the student's final results.
These systems are
Difficult to gather and combine data from different systems
Often no / poor integration (usually export/import xls or csv!!!)
Solution: Extract data from multiple sources and upload into a central Data Warehouse.

Data from Multiple databases
Imagine that I want to compare student data originating from two different University computer systems.
Here are some of the problems:
Different DBMSs (Oracle vs SQL Server vs …)
Different apps / software (StudentOne vs ??? vs …)
Different table / column names
Different data types
Different meanings of words (semester, teaching period, unit, course, subject, lab, class, tutorial, seminar )
Different unit codes / names (how to identify database units)
Different conventions (1st day of week is Monday / Sunday???)
Different granularity. One university records all assignment scores, test and exams. The other records final score and grade.

ETL (extract transform load)
ETL is the process used to transfer data from on system to another
Performed by code you have written in PL/SQL / C# / VB / PHP…or by DBMS ETL tools with a 'rule' interface
Data may have to be transformed
Data values may be missing (e.g. missing postcode, purchase date)
Measurements / Currencies converted to single type
Data values may require conversion (0 to Male, 1 to Female)
Customer 0001 in NSW may also be Customer 9999 in Vic
ETL applies a set rules to data during the transfer process
Exclude data where crucial data is missing
Fix / convert data is possible
Aggregate / Summarize data (105 separate purchases of CocaCola from a single store may be converted to a single row for that store)

Business Intelligence & Data
Many businesses attempt to overcome some problems by using an Enterprise System (such as SAP or Microsoft Dynamics or …)
A Enterprise system is a huge customizable software solution that tries to contain all software apps for a business (Sales, CRM, Inventory Management, Transport & Delivery, Manufacturing…) .
The idea is that a system developed by a single developer won't have ETL issues.  All data is correct and compatible.
Even so, mergers & acquisitions are common place in business. Soon we may have two enterprise systems!!!!
All of the same ETL and compatibility problems arise.
Solution: Extract data from multiple sources and upload into a central Data Warehouse

Business Intelligence & Data
Different users have different uses for data requirements.
Operational Staff   vs  Business Analysts
Operational staff need 'low-level' information
E.g. How many Books are required in order #1234 that is being shipped today?
These queries interact with few rows of the DB
Business Analysts & Managers need 'high-level' aggregated information
E.g. Are sales of Books increasing or decreasing over the last three years?
Are there changes in the genres/style of Books being ordered?
They use analysis tools like Power BI

Data Warehouses
A Data Warehouse have a small collection of tables.
Data is stored in 'cubes' (typically made up of 4 or so tables)
Most data in a TPS is not transferred to DW
We don't need to know that
Jim Smith was the driver who delivered order #123
Sue Jones enrolled in a class on Mon 8.30 but changed to Wed 12:30 class after week 2 of semester
We only transfer data that will be used by Analysts and Senior Business managers.

Typical Data Warehouse
Data Warehouses
Advantages:
Relieves stress on TPSs
Quick processing speed
Simple schemas (Far fewer tables. Far fewer columns)
Easy to write queries/reports
Disadvantages:
Many months / years to build
Need to anticipate what questions analysts will ask
Inflexible

Data Warehouses
A cube in a DW has two type of table
One Fact Tables (or Measure Table)
Contains the data that users want to quantify, measure & aggregate
Sales
Enrolments
Visits
A few Dimension Tables
Essentially a 'lookup table'.
The customer details
The stock item details

Dimensional Model
The fact and dimension tables is often called a star schema.
Dimension Tables
Fact Table

Dimension Tables
The dimension tables contain descriptive attributes
Often these based on ‘strong entity’ from an ERD
Typical tables: Customer, Product, Student, Region…
Think of a DimensionTables as being ‘Lookup Table’
CUSTOMER (Cust_Id, CustName, City, RegionName, RegionManager)
PRODUCT   (Product_Id, ProdName, ProdCategory, Price, Tax)

Fact Tables
Fact tables most often contain ‘transactional’ data
Data such as Events & Activities
Sales, Purchases, Movements, Results…
Often these based on ‘weak entity’ from an ERD
Data in the Fact Tables refer to dimension tables
E.g.  Each Fact will refer to a row in each of the dimension tables
SALES (Cust_Id, Product_Id, SaleDate, Qty, SellingPrice)
This Fact refers to data in these dimension tables:
Customer
Product
Date

Dimension Tables are not normalized
Data stored in a DW dimension table is not normalized
Usually 2nd Normal Form – has non-key dependencies
It contains redundant data
CUSTOMER (Cust_Id, CustName, City, RegionName, RegionManager )
137	Fred Jones	Melbourne	 South	Glen Jones
141	Sue Davies	Adelaide	 South         Wendy Hull
144	Di Harris	Geelong	 South         Emma Wilks
151	Hugh Potts	Sydney		 North	Alan Morse
173	Ted Grub	Melbourne	 South	Glen Jones
181	Helen Ward	Geelong	 South         Emma Wilks
201	Jim Taylor	Sydney		 North 	Alan Morse
219	Clare Toms	 Melbourne 	 South 	Glen Jones

Data Warehouse information
The Fact table of a Data warehouses can be very large
Aussie Bank
US Bank
Chinese Bank
Large distributed storage systems are required
Storing and retrieving tables with large amounts of data is a specialized area of expertise
Need to know about the data and the relationships within the data
Domain Knowledge Experts just as important than Database Experts
